content: If you're facing a deadline panic, use the 'Two-Minute Rule'. Start any daunting academic task for just two minutes. By lowering the cognitive threshold for engagement, you often bypass procrastination anxiety.

Experiencing test anxiety? Before entering the exam hall, do physiological sighs: two quick inhales through the nose followed by a long exhale through the mouth. This rapidly lowers autonomic arousal.

If you've received a failing grade, remember it does not define your worth. Access your university's 'Academic Success Center' or talk to your professor during office hours-they almost always have a recovery plan mapped out for students.

When studying for finals, prioritize recall over recognition. Actively pulling information out of your brain (e.g. via flashcards or practice tests) builds far stronger neural pathways than simply re-reading lecture slides.

During an acute panic attack, hold an ice cube or splash freezing water on your face. This activates the mammalian dive reflex, forcing your parasympathetic nervous system to abruptly slow your heart rate.

For generalized anxiety, try 'Worry Time'. Schedule precisely 15 minutes a day at 4:00 PM to furiously write down every worry. If you worry outside that time, tell yourself 'I will think about this at 4 PM'.
When spiraling in anxious thoughts, use the 5-4-3-2-1 grounding technique: identify 5 things you see, 4 you can touch, 3 you hear, 2 you smell, and 1 you taste.
Imposter syndrome thrives in silence. Speak to a trusted peer or mentor about feeling 'like a fraud'. You will almost universally find that even the most successful academics feel the exact same way.
Create a 'Hype Folder'. Whenever you receive a compliment, a good grade, or positive feedback, save it in a specific folder. Read it whenever you feel unqualified to be in your program.
If you are having a severe crisis or thoughts of self-harm, immediately call the National Suicide Prevention Lifeline at 988. They are totally free, anonymous, and available 24/7.
For immediate crisis intervention via text, message HOME to 741741. A trained crisis counselor will respond within minutes to help you de-escalate.
If you are residing on campus and feel unsafe, dial your campus police department or 911 immediately. They can request a mental health response unit.

For recurring issues related to academic stress, clinical psychology heavily recommends that you practice cognitive reframing to stabilize autonomic arousal. Doing this for just 10 minutes a day has been empirically shown to improve student outcomes.
